meta.name: RRS Ruminative Response Scale
meta.description: The RRS is a 22-item self-report measure of ruminative responses to depressed mood, including Brooding and Reflection subscales.
meta.author: Nolen-Hoeksema & Morrow (1991)
meta.reference: Nolen-Hoeksema S, Morrow J. A prospective study of depression and posttraumatic stress symptoms after a natural disaster. J Pers Soc Psychol. 1991;61(1):115-121.
meta.instruction: Please indicate whether you almost never, sometimes, often, or almost always think or do each one when you feel down, sad, or depressed.

questions.text: Think about how alone you feel
Think "I won't be able to do my job if I don't snap out of this"
Think about your feelings of fatigue and achiness
Think about how hard it is to concentrate
Think "What am I doing to deserve this?"
Think about how passive and unmotivated you feel
Analyze recent events to try to understand why you are depressed
Think about how you don't seem to feel anything anymore
Think "Why can't I get going?"
Think "Why do I always react this way?"
Go away by yourself and think about why you feel this way
Write down what you are thinking about and analyze it
Think about a recent situation, wishing it had gone better
Think "I won't be able to concentrate if I keep feeling this way"
Think "Why do I have problems other people don't have?"
Think "Why can't I handle things better?"
Think about how sad you feel
Think about all your shortcomings, failings, faults, mistakes
Think about how you don't feel up to doing anything
Analyze your personality to try to understand why you are depressed
Go someplace alone to think about your feelings
Think about how angry you are with yourself

ranges.label: Low Rumination
suggestions: Maintain awareness of your thought patterns

ranges.label: Mild Rumination
ranges.description: You show mild levels of ruminative thinking.
suggestions: Practice redirecting attention from repetitive negative thinking
Engage in physical activity or hobbies when feeling down

ranges.label: Moderate Rumination
ranges.description: You show moderate levels of ruminative thinking, which may prolong depressive episodes.

ranges.label: High Rumination
ranges.description: Your rumination level is high and may warrant professional attention.
suggestions: Cognitive behavioral therapy or mindfulness-based therapy can be effective
Build a strong social support network
